title: Topic 202: Why Players Name Characters After Themselves: Identity in Avatars
content:
# Topic 202: Why Players Name Characters After Themselves: Identity in Avatars

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the curious phenomenon of players who, when presented with a universe of infinite fantastical possibilities, choose to name their digital counterparts after... themselves. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this seemingly mundane choice, from the subtle whispers of self-perception theory to the grand pronouncements of self-discrepancy theory. Through a rigorous (and occasionally ridiculous) analysis of academic research, real-world examples, and the collective wisdom of internet forums, we argue that naming a character "Steve" in a world of dragons and demons is not a failure of imagination, but a profound statement of identity. This study will demonstrate that the choice to be oneself, even in a virtual world, is a fascinating intersection of narcissism, a desire for authenticity, and a subconscious effort to bridge the gap between the real and the digital. Or maybe, just maybe, they couldn't think of anything better.

## INTRODUCTION

The character creation screen. A digital womb, pregnant with possibility. Here, you can be anyone. A towering Orc with a penchant for poetry. A nimble Elf who secretly enjoys heavy metal. A grizzled space marine with a heart of gold and a crippling fear of butterflies. The options are limitless. And yet, after an hour of tweaking nose sliders and agonizing over horn-to-tusk ratios, a significant portion of the gaming population arrives at a name for their magnificent creation: their own. 

Yes, in a world where you can be "Xylar the Unconquered," many choose to be "Kevin from Accounting." This paper seeks to understand this baffling, yet common, phenomenon. Why, when given the chance to escape the mundane, do so many players choose to drag it with them into the virtual realm? Is it a simple lack of creativity? A deep-seated need for self-affirmation? Or is it a quiet act of rebellion against the very escapism that video games promise? 

We will explore the psychological drivers behind this decision, from the desire to create an idealized yet familiar version of oneself, to the subtle ways our virtual avatars influence our real-world behavior. We will journey through the uncanny valley of self-representation, armed with academic theories and humorous anecdotes, to uncover the truth behind the age-old question: to be, or not to be... yourself, in a video game. This exploration is not merely an academic exercise; it offers a window into the evolving nature of identity in the digital age and provides valuable insights for anyone interested in human behavior, from game developers to marketers, and even to Kevin from Accounting himself, as he embarks on his next epic adventure as... Kevin.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the matter of digital doppelgangers. While the specific act of naming a character after oneself has been criminally understudied (a gap this paper bravely attempts to fill), a wealth of research exists on the broader topics of avatar creation and player identity. This body of work, when viewed through a sufficiently humorous lens, provides a solid foundation for our investigation.

A cornerstone of this field is the work of Nick Yee, a man who has spent more time studying virtual worlds than most of us have spent in the real one. Yee’s research has consistently shown that players tend to create “slightly idealized” versions of themselves [1]. This isn't just about vanity; it's about creating a more effective and enjoyable play experience. After all, who wouldn't want to be a slightly taller, wittier, and more athletically gifted version of themselves? It’s like real life, but with better stats.

Building on this, we have the grandly named “Proteus Effect,” a term coined by Yee and his colleague Jeremy Bailenson [1]. Named after the shape-shifting Greek god, the Proteus Effect describes how our in-game behavior changes to match the appearance of our avatar. If your avatar is tall and imposing, you’re more likely to be assertive. If your avatar is attractive, you’re more likely to be sociable and self-disclosing. This suggests that naming your character after yourself might be a subconscious attempt to ground your virtual self, to prevent the Proteus Effect from running amok and turning you into a completely different person. Or, more cynically, it’s a way to ensure that your idealized self’s triumphs are still *your* triumphs.

Then we have the psychological heavyweight: Self-Discrepancy Theory (SDT). As explored by Loewen, Burris, and Nacke, SDT provides a surprisingly robust framework for understanding avatar creation [2]. The theory posits that we are constantly navigating the gap between our “actual self” (who we are), our “ideal self” (who we want to be), and our “ought self” (who we think we should be). Their research found that players with a large gap between their actual and ideal selves were more likely to create idealized avatars. Meanwhile, those with a large gap between their actual and ought selves were more likely to create completely different, fantastical characters. This suggests that naming a character after oneself might be an act of self-acceptance, a declaration that the gap between one's actual and ideal self is not so large as to require a complete overhaul. Or, it could be a sign of a very small imagination. The jury is still out.

## ANALYSIS

So, what drives a person to enter a world of myth and magic, and introduce themselves as ‘Dave’? The reasons are as varied and complex as the character creation menus themselves. Let's dissect this digital identity crisis with the precision of a level 80 rogue.

**The Comfort of the Familiar:** In a world of bewildering new rules, complex lore, and potentially hostile online communities, having a familiar name can be a comforting anchor. It’s one less thing to remember, one less layer of fiction to maintain. When you’re trying to figure out how to slay a dragon, you don’t want to be bogged down by the existential dread of remembering whether you’re ‘Zarthor’ or ‘Xylos’. You’re Dave. You hit things with a sword. Simple.

**The Idealized Self, Perfected:** As Yee’s research suggests, we like to create idealized versions of ourselves [1]. But what is the ultimate ideal, if not a version of *you* that can shoot fireballs from your hands? By naming the character after yourself, you are not just creating an idealized avatar; you are creating an idealized *you*. This isn't just 'a hero'; it's 'me, but with a +5 Sword of Awesomeness'. This creates a powerful psychological link, making victories feel more personal and defeats more… well, let's not talk about defeats. This is an idealized version, after all.

**A Touch of Narcissism:** Let's be honest, there's a healthy dose of narcissism in all of us. And what's more narcissistic than wanting to be the hero of your own story? By naming your character after yourself, you are literally inserting yourself into the narrative. You are the chosen one, the savior of the realm, the one who will finally defeat the evil overlord. And your name is Steve. This isn't just escapism; it's wish-fulfillment of the highest order.

**The 'Authenticity' Argument:** In an age of digital anonymity and carefully curated online personas, using your real name can be seen as an act of authenticity. It’s a declaration that you are not hiding behind a fictional identity. You are who you are, both in the real world and the virtual one. This is particularly prevalent in games that have a strong social component, where building trust and relationships is key. It’s harder to be a jerk online when your name isn’t ‘xX_DragonSlayer_Xx’, but ‘Bob from next door’.

**The Failure of Imagination:** And then, of course, there's the simplest explanation of all: some people just aren't very creative. After spending hours crafting the perfect jawline and selecting the most intimidating set of eyebrows, their creative juices are simply depleted. In a moment of exhausted resignation, they type in their own name, click ‘enter’, and begin their epic journey as ‘Susan the Barbarian’. And you know what? There’s a certain purity in that. It’s a testament to the fact that sometimes, the most straightforward solution is the best one. Or at least, the easiest.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The digital wilderness is rife with examples of players grappling with the existential crisis of the character creation screen. A cursory glance at any gaming forum will reveal countless threads with titles like, “What’s the best character name you’ve ever seen?” or “Why do people use their real names?” These digital campfires are where the collective unconscious of the gaming community is laid bare, and they provide a treasure trove of anecdotal evidence for our investigation.

One of the most common justifications for self-naming, echoed in countless Reddit threads, is the simple desire for friends to be able to easily identify them. In the chaotic world of online multiplayer games, where you might be playing with dozens of other people, being able to quickly spot ‘Bob’ in a crowd of ‘ShadowGamer92s’ is a practical advantage. It’s a digital equivalent of wearing a brightly colored hat in a crowd, a simple and effective way of saying, “Here I am!”

Then there are the stories of players who have taken the concept of the idealized self to its logical extreme. Take, for example, the countless players of *The Sims* who have meticulously recreated their own homes, families, and even pets in the game. They then proceed to live out their ideal lives, giving themselves promotions, buying lavish furniture, and generally living a life of virtual luxury. In this context, naming the character after oneself is not just a choice; it’s a necessity. It’s the only way to truly experience the vicarious thrill of a life where you can afford a swimming pool and your kids don’t talk back.

Of course, there are also the cautionary tales. The internet is littered with stories of players who, in a moment of misguided authenticity, used their full real name as their character name, only to find themselves the target of online harassment or doxxing. This serves as a stark reminder that the line between the real and the virtual is becoming increasingly blurred, and that the decision to use one’s real name in a game is not without its risks. It’s a digital tightrope walk between authenticity and anonymity, and not everyone makes it to the other side.

And let’s not forget the unintentional comedy. The player who named their hulking, muscle-bound barbarian ‘Gary’, not as a joke, but because they genuinely couldn’t think of anything else. The guild of grizzled, battle-hardened warriors, whose names are Dave, John, and Mike. These are the unsung heroes of the gaming world, the ones who remind us that sometimes, the most memorable characters are the ones who are the most unexpectedly, and hilariously, mundane.

## DISCUSSION

What can we learn from the ‘Steves’ and ‘Daves’ of the gaming world? The implications of this seemingly trivial choice extend far beyond the confines of the character creation screen. They offer a fascinating glimpse into the evolving nature of identity in the digital age, and provide valuable insights for a wide range of fields, from game design to marketing.

For game developers, the takeaway is clear: customization is key. The more options players have to create an avatar that reflects their desired identity, the more engaged and immersed they will be. This doesn’t just mean more hairstyles and tattoos; it means providing a spectrum of options, from the wildly fantastical to the reassuringly mundane. The player who wants to be a ten-foot-tall lizard-man should be just as catered to as the player who wants to be a slightly more handsome version of themselves.

For marketers, the implications are more subtle, but no less significant. The rise of the ‘authentic’ online persona, as exemplified by the use of real names in games, suggests a growing desire for transparency and honesty in the digital realm. Consumers are becoming increasingly savvy to the artifice of online marketing, and are drawn to brands and influencers who project a sense of genuineness. The player who proudly names their character ‘Bob’ is, in a small way, part of a larger cultural shift towards a more authentic and less curated online experience.

And for the rest of us, the humble students of human behavior, the phenomenon of self-naming in video games is a rich and fertile ground for study. It is a microcosm of the larger questions of identity that we all grapple with. Who are we? Who do we want to be? And how do we bridge the gap between the two? The answers, it seems, may be found not in the grand pronouncements of philosophers, but in the quiet, and often humorous, choices we make on a character creation screen.

## CONCLUSION

In the grand tapestry of human behavior, the choice to name a video game character after oneself may seem like a minor thread. And yet, as we have seen, it is a thread that is interwoven with some of the most fundamental questions of identity, self-perception, and the evolving relationship between the real and the virtual. From the subtle narcissism of the idealized self to the pragmatic desire for a familiar anchor in a strange new world, the reasons for this choice are as diverse as the players themselves.

So, the next time you encounter a ‘Jennifer the Just’ or a ‘Brian the Brave’ in your online adventures, do not be too quick to judge. They are not simply uncreative souls who couldn’t be bothered to think of a more fantastical name. They are pioneers of a new form of identity, one that is both deeply personal and playfully performative. They are the heroes of their own stories, and they are not afraid to let the world know it. And in a world that is becoming increasingly virtual, there is something to be said for that kind of unapologetic self-representation. Even if your name is Gary.

## REFERENCES

[1] The Psychology of Video Game Avatars. (2013, November 29). The Psychology of Games. Retrieved from https://www.psychologyofgames.com/2013/11/the-psychology-of-video-game-avatars/

[2] Loewen, M. G. H., Burris, C. T., & Nacke, L. E. (2021). Me, Myself, and Not-I: Self-Discrepancy Type Predicts Avatar Creation Style. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *11*. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2020.01902
